title: practical english usage - Part 15
content:
name of a particular person, place, organisation, etc. Examples: Alex, Brazil; proper noun or proper name  a noun (most often with no article) which  is the following noun which  has been left out. Example: I'll take these.

can also be used for a determiner when this includes the meaning of a noun or noun phrase (like the cat, Tom's self, the family's). The  word pronoun pronoun  a word like it, yourself, their, which  is used instead of a more precise progressive (or continuous) infinitive  a form like to be going; to be waiting.

Language terminology •  xxviii (a meal); drive (a car); give (a present). See also intransitive.

transitive  a transitive verb is one that can have an object. Examples: eat third person  see person.

its form and/or the use of an auxiliary. Examples: worked, saw, will go; is sitting.

tense  a verb form that shows the time of an action, event or state, by a change in can't swim, can you?; Very noisy, those kids. See also question tag.

of a sentence, especially in speech. Examples: He likes to talk, Josh does; You tag  a short phrase (e.g. pronoun subject + auxiliary verb) added on to the end /'kæpɪtəlɪz(ə)m/,   button /'bʌtn/).

I, m  and n can act as syllables (for instance in the words bottle /'batl/, capitalism consonants before and/or after the vowel. Sometimes the consonant sounds category has four. A  syllable normally has a vowel, and usually one or more syllable  The  word cat has one syllable, cattle has two, cataract has three and same way (e.g. most intelligent, most politely).

(e.g. oldest, fastest); also the structure most + adjective/adverb, used in the superlative the form of an adjective or adverb made with the suffix -est understandable; Chinese. See also prefix.

word to give an additional or different meaning. Examples: climatology; suffix  a form like -ology, -able or -ese, which  can be added to the end of a rest of its sentence. Examples: when, if, because.

subordinating conjunction  a conjunction that joins a subordinate clause to the you wherever you go. See also clause, main clause.

Examples: I thought that you understood; What I need is a drink; I'll follow example as subject, object or adverbial in the main clause of a sentence.

subordinate clause  a clause which  functions as part of another clause, for immediately; We prefer that he pay in cash.

structures. Examples: If I were you...; It's important that he be informed subjunctive  a verb form (not very common in British English) used in certain Oil floats on water. See also object.

action that the verb refers to. Examples: Ellie gave me a wonderful smile; affirmative clause. It often says (in an active clause) who  or what does the subject  a noun phrase or pronoun that normally comes before the verb in an can (/kæn/, /kən/), was(/woz/, /wəz/),for (/fɔ: (r)/, /fə(r)/).

quicker pronunciation with the vowel /ə/   or /ɪ/   (weak form). Examples: slowly and carefully with the vowel that is written (strong form), or with a strong form, weak form  Certain words can be pronounced in two ways: stresses (WHERE'S the NEW SEcretary?).

(parTIcular); in the sentence Where's the new secretary? there are three higher pitch. In the word particular, the main stress is on the second syllable made to sound more important than the rest, by using a louder voice and/or stress  the way in which  one or more parts of a word, phrase or sentence are I'm cold; Daniel didn't come home last night.

statement  a sentence which  gives information; not a question. Examples: I'm not is standard English; I ain't is non-standard.

accepted for use in government, the law, business, education and literature.

standard  A  standard form of a language is the one that is most generally infinitives 'incorrect,' but they are common in standard usage.

of the infinitive. Example: to easily understand. Some people consider split split infinitive  a structure in which  an adverb comes between to and the rest Language terminology •  xxix weak form  see strong form.
